# Questions in Livestream 64

Live Stream #64: A Flock of Horus Birds.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:12</a> | How do I respond to skeptics who say Abraham could not have met Philistine kings because the Philistines did not exist yet? | He treats the Philistine references in Genesis as retrojection: a later author uses familiar contemporary terms for earlier settings rather than claiming Philistines were there in the full later historical sense. | He explains that the question bundles several issues, including Ur of the Chaldees and the Philistine references, but focuses on the Genesis language about Philistine kings. His answer is that Genesis can use the author's contemporary place or people names for earlier events, much as modern speakers use later names for older regions. On that reading, the text need not require fully developed later Philistines in Abraham's day; it can identify the setting for the reader with a later, familiar term. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=792" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:12</a> | Is the wall at Karnak from Shoshenq's time inscribed with names like the Fields of Abraham and the Highlands of David? | He does not think so from a quick scan, but he explicitly says he did not do enough preparatory work to confirm or deny it and would want a deeper look at the toponym list. | He identifies the relevant monument as the Bubastite Portal and tries to check an Oriental Institute translation during the stream. He says he doubts those exact names are on the list, though he notes that a Judah-related entry is present and that the date makes the claim at least worth checking. Because he had not prepared the toponym list in advance, he leaves the answer provisional and says he would need a more careful review before confirming or denying the claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:15</a> | What advice do you have for new believers looking for a church in a time of apostasy? | He recommends looking for a church with serious mentoring and discipleship, because many churches do better at baptism and worship culture than at deep teaching and formation. | He frames the problem through the Great Commission: churches are often competent at baptism and worship, but weak at making disciplined followers and teaching believers deeply. For new believers, he recommends seeking a church with real mentoring, small-group discipleship, and instruction in how to read Scripture and think biblically. He contrasts that with emotion-driven worship alone, which may be meaningful but does not by itself give new believers the deep roots they need. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1400" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:20</a> | In your Mount Ebal video, did you make a mistake about the second scarab carrying the name of Ramesses? | Yes, he says he made that mistake, but argues the scarab is still identifiable as a Ramesside type by its style and published parallels. | He openly says he made the mistake: the name of Ramesses II does not appear on the first scarab. He then points to Baruch Brandl's publication of the Mount Ebal scarabs, arguing that the first scarab still belongs to a Ramesside type because the parallels are dated to the 19th Dynasty and later Ramesside contexts. So the correction changes the inscription claim, but not his broader dating point about the scarab's type. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:12</a> | How do we know the Egyptians did not boil water to make it safe for drinking? | He says they certainly could boil water, but there is no evidence that they did so specifically for drinking water, and beer was a more practical processed drink in their context. | He distinguishes ability from evidence: Egyptians had vessels and cooking practices that show they could boil water, but that does not show they routinely boiled drinking water. He argues beer made more practical sense because it required less fuel than full boiling, added nutrients, tasted better, and itself functioned as a processed safer drink. Fuel was costly, and once boiled water cooled it could still become unappealing or contaminated, so he sees beer rather than plain boiled water as the better-attested practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1842" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:42</a> | Why did Abram say Sarah was his sister, didn't Egyptian pharaohs sometimes practice incest, and was Sarah really his half-sister? | He says Abram lied out of fear, pharaonic incest happened mainly in succession-crisis contexts, and Sarah was likely a more distant clan relative than a literal half-sister. | He answers the three parts separately. Abram lied because he feared Pharaoh would kill him and take Sarah, so the claim was self-protective. Egyptian royal incest did occur, but he says it was not normal and was especially associated with succession crises and ideas about royal blood. On Sarah's relationship to Abram, he argues the genealogy may allow a broader kinship category, so she could have been a niece or second cousin rather than a literal half-sister in the modern sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:50</a> | If you could ask God one question in heaven, what would you ask? | He says he would ask for access to God's library. | He gives a light answer rather than a doctrinal one: he would ask for the keys to God's library and enough time to read it. He jokes about wanting an information download, but the substance is that his desired heavenly question is really a request for full access to divine knowledge and records. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2397" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:57</a> | What is the origin of the expression "sons of Belial"? | He says it originally means worthless people or sons without value, with later demonological readings developing in the Second Temple period. | He traces the phrase to a Hebrew expression usually rendered "sons of Belial," meaning sons of worthlessness or people without value. He says the term is old, appearing in early biblical contexts such as Judges and possibly the Pentateuch. Later, especially in Second Temple interpretation, Belial begins to be treated more like a demonological figure, but that is a later development from the earlier idiom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2567" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:47</a> | In Isaiah 45:13, does the grammar point to Cyrus rebuilding Jerusalem, and is there amphiboly or messianic layering in the verse? | He says the passage does involve ambiguity: Cyrus is in view, but he also sees a broader messianic fulfillment running through the theological discourse. | He reads the wider context first, noting that Isaiah 45 explicitly names Cyrus as the Lord's anointed and addresses him as the one God raises up. Verse 13 therefore has Cyrus in view, but he also sees the surrounding discourse moving through broader theological claims about the Lord, righteousness, and deliverance. His answer preserves both levels: Cyrus is the immediate historical agent, while the passage can also carry a larger messianic or theological layering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:51</a> | How could Zerah the Ethiopian march against Asa if he would have had to pass through Egypt? | He argues Zerah was probably a famous Cushite general operating for Osorkon I, so the march would have happened with Egyptian approval, not against it. | He places the episode under Egypt's 22nd Dynasty, specifically Osorkon I, and argues that "Zerah" is not an Egyptian, Hebrew, or Semitic name. He suggests the biblical text may be naming a well-known Cushite general rather than presenting an independent Ethiopian invasion through hostile Egypt. On that reconstruction, Zerah's movement through Egypt is not a problem because he is acting within or with the Egyptian political-military framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:15</a> | In 1 Kings 20:16, does Ben-hadad's coalition of 32 kings literally mean 32 major heads of state? | He says no; these were probably many small city-state rulers, so the number sounds bigger than the actual scale of each polity. | He explains that Aram at this time was not a single unified state but a collection of smaller city-states after the breakup of earlier regional powers. In that setting, "32 kings" does not mean 32 large national monarchs comparable to modern heads of state. It likely refers to local rulers of small polities forming a coalition under Ben-hadad, a pattern he compares to other ancient coalitions of small kings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:42</a> | Why did the Babylonian 60-based time system survive into the present day? | He says the picture is mixed: Egypt contributed the 24-hour day, Babylonia the 60-minute hour, and later Europeans applied the same geometrical logic to seconds. | He corrects the premise by splitting the history of timekeeping into several pieces. The 24-hour day comes from Egypt and the water clock tradition, while Babylonian sexagesimal thinking explains the division of the hour into 60 minutes. Seconds, in his explanation, come later when early modern European clocks became precise enough to require a smaller division and borrowed the same geometry-based sexagesimal logic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3677" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:17</a> | Do you think the Trojan War was a real event, and if so when and why did it happen? | Yes; he places it broadly around 1260 to 1180 BC and interprets it as agricultural and expansionary conflict tied to wider Late Bronze Age upheaval. | He accepts the Trojan War as a real event and dates it broadly to the 1260-1180 BC window. He connects it to the same Late Bronze Age pressures that produced Sea Peoples movements, saying Greek peoples were pushing into western Anatolia for agricultural land and food resources. In his reconstruction, Troy was tied to the Hittite world, and the war reflects the Hittite Empire's weakening under both internal and external pressure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3854" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:14</a> | Did ancient Near Eastern cultures think rival nations' gods were real, evil, or accurately described? | He says polytheistic cultures often accepted, equated, borrowed, or even stole other peoples' gods rather than simply treating them as evil fictions. | He says most ancient Near Eastern polytheists gave some deference to other peoples' gods rather than treating them as unreal. He points to mixed worship contexts such as Avaris, where Levantine deities appear alongside Egyptian gods, and to examples of conquerors taking another people's divine statue. Israel was different because it tended to destroy rival divine images, but in broader polytheistic systems borrowing, equating, or relocating gods was common. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:21</a> | In Judges, what verbal clues help tell whether judges ruled in succession or concurrently in different places? | He says context is the key, especially shifts in place, tribe, oppression, and the presence or absence of explicit temporal markers. | He does not give a single mechanical marker; he says context is the main tool. If the narrative changes place, tribe, oppressor, or political setting without an explicit phrase of temporal succession, he thinks readers should be open to concurrent or parallel episodes. By contrast, clearer sequencing language such as a stated period of peace followed by a new judge gives stronger grounds for succession. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4221" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:21</a> | What are your thoughts on the eruv wires around places like Manhattan? | He describes them as a creative legal workaround that uses the letter of later sabbath interpretation to create a symbolic enclosed area where carrying is allowed. | He explains an eruv as a later Jewish legal construction based on defining an enclosed area through posts and a beam, with a wire serving as the beam. Since carrying within one's own house is allowed under that interpretation, the eruv symbolically treats a larger district as an enclosed space. He calls it a way of using the letter of the law to permit carrying within a defined sabbath boundary, including very large urban areas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:54</a> | Is dividing Old Testament laws into cultural, moral, and cultic categories a helpful method or just a trick to dismiss laws people dislike? | He thinks it is imperfect but legitimate when done with context, since not all laws function the same way and many were tied to temple practice. | He says no categorization of Old Testament law is perfect, but the distinction can still be useful. He stresses that many laws functioned as case law or guidance for judges, and that many commandments were tied specifically to temple, tabernacle, sacrifice, and ceremonial practice. The categories become irresponsible if used to discard disliked laws casually, but he does not see them as inherently a cheap trick because laws have different contexts and functions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:34</a> | In Judges 1:7, were Adoni-bezek's seventy kings really heads of families or more like village rulers? | He says they were probably rulers of many small towns or villages, and some could even represent repeat conquests of the same places. | He reads the passage as referring to kings of small settlements rather than mere family heads. Because ancient agrarian populations were spread through many small villages and towns, he thinks seventy local rulers in a region is plausible. He also allows that the number could include repeated conquest of the same town after rebellion, where a new installed ruler was later defeated and added to Adoni-bezek's tally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5071" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:31</a> | What were the grievous infirmities of Egypt in Deuteronomy 7:15? | He treats it as a broad reference to the severe disease environment of Egypt rather than one precisely identifiable illness. | He says the verse does not name one specific disease, so he treats it as a broad bucket for Egypt's known health burdens. He mentions polluted Nile water, parasites, intestinal disease, lesions, sores, flies, mosquito-borne illnesses, sexually transmitted diseases, malnutrition, arthritis, tooth decay, abscesses, and periodic plagues. The point is not a single diagnosis but the wider Egyptian disease environment that Israel knew from experience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:02</a> | What is going on with Gideon's ephod in Judges 8:27? | He says Gideon makes a cultic ritual garment he should not be making, and once displayed it is treated like idolatrous apparatus and becomes a snare. | He explains an ephod as a ritual garment, a vest or vest-kilt form associated with priestly and divine imagery. Gideon's problem is that he makes such a ritual object from the gold and displays it in Ophrah, where it becomes associated with idolatrous cult apparatus. Although the high priest had an ephod, Gideon had no business making this kind of object for himself, and in that cultural context people could easily treat it as a new idol or cultic focus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:14</a> | Are fortune cookies a form of divination, and should Christians avoid them? | He says no, not unless someone is treating them seriously as divination; ordinarily they are just playful sayings in cookies. | He says fortune cookies are not divination in ordinary use because no one is appealing to a deity or serious spiritual power to reveal the future. Most fortunes are generic sayings or playful aphorisms that can be interpreted many ways. Christians should avoid using them as divination if they are actually treating them as guidance for the future, but otherwise he sees them as harmless and unserious. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:32</a> | In Exodus 25:9, is the tabernacle or its furniture just a scale model of a larger heavenly structure? | He does not find the simple scale-model reading convincing and thinks the sacred furniture also functioned as a real point of divine-human interface, not merely as a miniature. | He reads Exodus 25:9 in the context of the tabernacle and ark instructions and says the dimensions of the ark are reasonable for an actual ritual object, not merely a reduced model. He grants that the mercy seat or sacred furniture may in some sense represent a larger divine throne room, much as ancient sacred objects could be models. But because the Shekinah glory was understood to be present between the cherubim, he emphasizes function: the ark was a real interface between God and Israel, not just a scale model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=6198" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:18</a> | Does Job 33:22-24 have christological overtones, and what should we make of Elihu's role there? | He says interpreters are all over the map and he does not want to force an opinion; the christological reading is possible, but he preserves uncertainty. | He says interpretation of Elihu and Job 33 is highly contested. Some exegetes see christological overtones in the mediator and ransom language, while others view Elihu as an overconfident younger speaker whose role is ambiguous. Because Elihu is not condemned the way the other comforters are, but also because the passage remains difficult, he declines a firm answer and leaves the christological reading as possible rather than settled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=6466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:46</a> | Is the story that someone found a golden ass image in the Jerusalem temple a fabrication, or does it connect to Seth or Hyksos traditions? | He thinks it is ideological fabrication and does not believe the Jerusalem temple would have normally housed that kind of animal image. | He notes that Hyksos worship is associated with Baal in the evidence he cites, then treats the golden donkey story as polemical. In his view, Seleucid-era writers had ideological reasons to portray Judeans as corrupt or ridiculous, especially in struggles over control of the high priesthood. He also argues that Jerusalem's temple culture was acutely sensitive to animal imagery, as shown by reactions to Herod's Roman eagle, so a normal golden animal image inside the temple is unlikely, though he leaves open the remote possibility of unrelated looted treasure near the precinct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=6786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:06</a> | What is the best explanation for the discrepancy between Jesus' genealogies in Matthew and Luke? | He declines a firm answer because genealogies often serve non-obvious literary and theological purposes, and he has not studied those two closely enough yet. | He says he has not examined Matthew's and Luke's genealogies closely enough to adjudicate the discrepancy. Instead, he offers caution about genealogies generally: they are not always simple pedigree lists, but can communicate posterity, theological meaning, and literary purposes that are not immediately intuitive. Because genealogies contain interpretive traps, he refuses to give a confident explanation without closer study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=7274" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:14</a> | Did Pharisees in the New Testament period believe their traditions came directly from Moses, and when did the traditions of the elders become the oral law? | He says the written oral law emerges in the post-70 AD rabbinic period, and Pharisees likely distinguished those traditions from the Torah itself rather than treating every elder tradition as direct Mosaic dictation. | He answers the second part first: after the destruction of the temple in AD 70 and especially after later upheavals, rabbis began preserving traditions in writing because the temple system and Sadducean role had collapsed. He places the written development of Mishnah, Talmud, and related rabbinic material roughly from the second century onward. For the New Testament Pharisees, he suggests the traditions of the elders were important, but he does not present them as already identical to the later written oral law or as simply direct Mosaic dictation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=7618" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:58</a> | What relationship would Moshe have had to Ramesses? Was it really brotherly? | He says no; Moses was probably older by about a decade and shared parts of the same household world, but not in the close brotherly way often depicted. | He reconstructs the Ramesside family setting by noting that Ramesses I was already a grandfather when he became king, followed by Seti I and then Ramesses II. Ramesses II would have been raised in the royal household and spent time around Avaris, but Moses would likely have been older and not a same-age brother figure. So the relationship may have involved overlapping household and court circles, not the intimate brotherly relationship often shown in popular retellings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=8160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:00</a> | In Judges 8, why does Gideon kill the men of Penuel but not the men of Succoth when the offense seems similar? | He reads the difference as lying in the aftermath: Succoth seems to give up its leaders, while Penuel as a whole stands behind its resistance and tower, though he notes some speculation is involved. | He walks through Judges 8 and notes that both Succoth and Penuel refuse support to Gideon's exhausted men. He thinks the later difference may be that Succoth's leaders are identified and handed over for punishment, while Penuel's tower and community resistance indicate a broader corporate stand against Gideon. He presents this as a plausible explanation rather than a fully certain one, because the text does not spell out every detail. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=8772" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:12</a> | If I attend a local Messianic Jewish congregation, how do I avoid being "that Gentile"? | He says to behave normally and respectfully, avoid cosplay and overperforming Jewish identity, and be sensitive to congregational customs, especially around food. | Drawing on his own time working in a Messianic Jewish congregation, he warns against conspicuous overperformance: dressing up, adopting exaggerated markers of Jewish identity, or trying to out-Jewish the congregation. His practical advice is to act normally, respect the community's customs, and be considerate with issues such as food and kosher sensitivities. The core point is humility and sensitivity rather than performative identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=9453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:33</a> | Why is Pascha or Easter celebrated on Sunday rather than on a fixed calendar date? | He says Easter is tied to Sunday because of the resurrection, while different Christian traditions calculate the date differently in relation to the lunar calendar and Passover. | He says Easter is always on Sunday because Christians are celebrating the day Jesus rose from the dead. Passover follows the Jewish calendar and does not always fall on Sunday, while Christian liturgical calendars calculate Easter by lunar rules that he does not try to recite from memory. He also notes that Eastern and Western traditions can calculate the date differently, and Easter can sometimes coincide with Passover but does not have to. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=9827" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:47</a> | If Moshe had not parted the Reed Sea, why could the Israelites not just go around the Yam Suph? | He says they were boxed in and could not outrun Pharaoh's chariots; the route itself functions as a divine trap for the Egyptian army. | He describes the Yam Suph as part of a massive estuary at the Pelusiac branch of the Nile, thousands of square miles in extent though not very deep. The Israelites were between the sea, wilderness, and named locations, with Pharaoh's army effectively on top of them. They could not simply detour around such a large water system while being pursued by chariots; in his reading, the geography sets up the divine trap that destroys the Egyptian force. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:25</a> | Why do we not find chariots or evidence of them there after the crossing? | He says Egyptian chariots were built largely from wood and leather, so in that wet environment they would quickly fall apart and leave little to recover. | He says chariots would not preserve well because their main materials were wood and stretched leather. Once submerged and smashed around in flowing estuary water, the leather and wood would rot, bodies would be consumed or scattered, and material would wash downstream. Only small bronze fittings might remain, and those would be sparse, so he does not expect recoverable chariot evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:50</a> | How deep and wide was the Sea of Reeds? | He estimates it was hundreds of miles wide and about 20 to 40 feet deep, based on sediment deposits and geological surveys in the area. | He estimates the Sea of Reeds as hundreds of miles wide but only around 20 to 40 feet deep. He bases the depth estimate on sediment deposits and geological surveys in the region, including knowledge gained from the Suez Canal area. His point is that it was vast laterally even if not especially deep. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:54</a> | If the wind blew from the east, how would the sea have split, and would the people have had to fight hurricane-force winds? | He says the setdown effect would part the water roughly east to west and would not require hurricane winds, only about a strong 50 mph wind. | He connects the east wind to wind setdown studies in the eastern Nile region, where strong focused winds can move water and locally lower water levels. In his explanation, the water would be pushed roughly east to west, creating a crossing route from the southeastern side toward the southwestern side before the people turned toward the wilderness. For a 20- to 40-foot-deep body of water, he says the necessary wind would be about 50 mph: strong, but not hurricane-force. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:48</a> | Why do Saul and David's world contain so many El and Baal names and fewer Yahwistic ones? | He says the period reflects widespread idolatry and cultural fashion, so those naming patterns fit a time when Israel was not consistently faithful. | He links the naming pattern to the religious condition of the period coming out of Judges and into Samuel, when Israel was often disobedient and idolatry was widespread. In that context, people would naturally use more Baal and El names, with El also retaining some ambiguity because it could be associated with Yahweh. He also treats naming as partly cultural fashion, not a clean measure of formal theology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11006" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:26</a> | What resources are there for learning about continental young earth creationism? | He says he does not really have resources to recommend because that movement tends to be niche, local, and not strongly organized around publishing. | He contrasts continental young earth creationism with American young earth creationism. The American movement is evangelistic, conference-driven, and supported by publishing houses, literature, theme parks, and media, while the continental version tends to be local, niche, and less interested in public outreach. Because much of it circulates through churches, societies, tracts, photocopies, or borrowed American material, he does not have clear resources to recommend. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:32</a> | Cheddar or mozzarella? | He says it depends entirely on the application, and neither is his overall favorite cheese. | He answers by use case: cheddar for macaroni and cheese or cheese on toast, mozzarella for pizza, chicken Parmesan, and tomato-cucumber Italian salad. He says fresh mozzarella is wonderful, but neither cheddar nor mozzarella is his favorite. He names Cambazola as his favorite and also mentions liking Stilton and Manchego. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:35</a> | When new dynasties took the throne, how did they establish religious legitimacy after starting as ordinary people? | He says they often linked themselves to royal ancestry, cults, king lists, or revered royal figures, though those legitimacy projects were not always fully successful. | He gives several mechanisms for legitimacy: claiming descent from royal ancestors, inserting oneself into king lists, or venerating revered royal figures and their cults. He uses Seti I's Abydos king list as an example of presenting himself in continuity with earlier kings while excluding illegitimate ones, and Ramesses II's veneration of Ahmose-Nefertari as a way to attach himself to an older royal line. He adds that such efforts did not always solve political and priestly tensions, especially with Theban interests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11874" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:54</a> | Did biblical authors have room in their thinking for corporate or group persons? | Yes; he says clan and corporate responsibility are central to biblical thought, often more so than modern Western individualism expects. | He says biblical authors often think in terms of clans, family groups, tribes, and nations, not only isolated individuals. He cites the law about an unsolved murder outside a city, where city elders ritually deny responsibility and make a sacrifice, as evidence of corporate responsibility. He also points to Revelation's seven churches being addressed as corporate bodies and contrasts this with modern Western individualism, noting that clan societies can bear consequences for the actions of members. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=12256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:16</a> | How do I become a New Testament scholar coming out of high school? | He says to begin with a strong bachelor's program, get Greek early, and expect the long path of further graduate study and specialization. | He recommends starting with college, preferably a credible accredited bachelor's program in a humanities field such as philosophy or religious studies, while getting Greek as early as possible and also learning Hebrew. He describes the usual path as a bachelor's degree, then one or two master's degrees such as an MDiv, ThM, or MA sequence, followed by a PhD specialization in New Testament studies. He stresses that the goal is being trained to handle the New Testament competently and accurately, and that this takes time. |
